sentences.text: The words what the officer gave me-- when we will need you we will notify you.
And with that, we went out.
It's very hard to say for me.
But it's to understand, when people are coming, the nation is in war with German-- with Germany.
People are coming, they want to fight the Germans.
To get an answer like this, it's very hard to describe it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't want to have, most probably, Jews there, in the army.
The young people, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was if-- they were talking about this, that they are building up another line to fight, that's supposed to be on the River Vistula.
And so we decided to go over there.
Maybe we would be enlisted and we would fight the Germans.
The father said to me, oh, the best thing would be the young people, I can see the young people leaving the town, and they're going to the east.
So I believe that you make a good decision that I was there-- I don't think so that the will touch all the elderly Jews.
Mind you, that my father wasn't old at that time.
My father was born in 1882, in the [NON-ENGLISH], in '33-- 1939.
I went with my cousin, Izydor Zilbriszpitz.
I-Z-Y-D-O-R, Zilbriszpitz, Z-I-L-B-R-S-Z-P-I-T-Z.
I had a lot of my schoolmates, was a [INAUDIBLE], there's a lot of my schoolmates.
We went on bikes.
Some of them they were all right, but mine was a horrible one.
Push bikes.
We went through [NON-ENGLISH], one little town not far from Radom.
It was burning, the whole little town was burning.
I went to Lublin, from Lublin to-- one of the-- I forgot the name.
That was already on the River Bug, the one little town.
There were horrible things were happened.
We were so unlucky.
Wherever we went to a town, the bombardments was going on.
So we were flying, not riding the push bike, but flying, really.
When I left Radom, I got some money in the [NON-ENGLISH], some banks.
But everything was closed, and I went-- I left Radom without money, actually.
So that was very, very, very hard time for me.
Some of the schoolmates, my cousin, got some money and we were able to-- we was able to put through that bad time.
Horrible.
There was a lot [NON-ENGLISH], a lot of people.
They were leaving the places where they-- where they used to live, and just running away from the bombardment.
But they were-- with the [NON-ENGLISH], they were actually quicker than we are with the bikes.
Not so much on the road, but when we were trying to have a rest in the towns that we went through them, it was-- the bombardment was a horrible.
I end up, after [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] being in [NON-ENGLISH], being in Lutsk, in Kowel-- sorry-- in Lutsk, in Rowne.
And that was about eight or nine days for running away, we were on the border Poland and Russia.
The place was called Korzec.
K-O-R-Z-E-C.

And a day before, or two days before, the truck that was signed through Ribbentrop and Molotov, and the army-- just when we came to the-- to that town, little town, the army, the Russian army went over the border and went to-- I asked one of the [NON-ENGLISH] that's a very hard characteristic, and I will never forget this too-- I ask him-- I will say it in Russian-- [RUSSIAN]?
And he told me--
How far you are going?
So he said to me, [RUSSIAN].
That was a [RUSSIAN].
That we will be in Washington, to-- that will go so fast to get to Washington.
Anyway, he told me, I ask him, tell me, please, what's it that they are dividing Poland.
Remember the history of the-- that this will be occupied, half of it till Vistula, he told me that time.
And the Germans will be on the other side to show that Poland was divided in half.
But the end was that the-- the demarcation line was on the River Bug, B-U-G.
So I went back with them as they are.
When he told me that the-- that the demarcation line-- line will be Vistula, Vistula is not far from Radom.
So I was thinking to go closer to be [INAUDIBLE] and my father, to be there.
But when I find out that the demarcation line will be on the River Bug, I start to think I was without money to do something with my profession.
And from Kowel, I went to attempt back to Kowel.
From Kowel I decided to go to Bialystok, because Bialystok is on the other side of the Bug, the River Bug.
And after I decided to go to Bialystok, because there are some tanneries there, that I will be able to establish myself.
And I noticed right away that this war that would definitely take a long, long time.
Because I know the history of Poland, and they have been not once divided between Germany, Austria, Russia.
All by myself.
I went to one [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] gentleman, on the name Lazar Lewin.
L-A-Z-A-R, L-E-W-I-N.
He was a chemicalist.
He got some representations of firms from overseas and he used to sell to my father some chemicals and extracts.
When I came to his place, a lot of people like me have been.
The whole town was full and his flat was full.
They called them [NON-ENGLISH]-- that we are running away from the-- And I asked him, listen, is that possible, because I know that you are big contacts with tanneries here.
Is that possible-- he know me very well-- if that will be possible to-- I want to work in a tannery.
I want to engage, because it looks to me that I have to leave and I have to earn money to be able to leave.
And so he said to me, wait a second.
And he closed me in one of the rooms.
And he took, maybe, no longer than 10 minutes.
He came in with the gentleman, a young gentleman, with a [NON-ENGLISH], very nice person.
And he introduced me to him and said, that is my son.
He is a rabbi, a rav, a rabbi, in a little town in Motal.
My son is a rav, is a rabbi in Motal, at night, we heard that Vilno will be next as the capital, and from there-- and Lithuania is not in the war.
From there you can go wherever you want.
Listen to me, better go help my son, because the wife, his wife and the children are still there.
Go there and try to get to Vilna.
And I listened to him.
I went to that Motal, to Pinsk.
I remember because the trains no-- that is the end station of the train.
And after we have about four kilometers to the famous swamps-- Pinskiya baloty.
Swamps, you know, how to call it in English?
Not in the Russian-- in the-- they-- they have to be, you know, a place through the forest, you know, to go-- where the boots are sinking in the--
Swamps, swamps.
That's correct.
No.
Lewin, but I don't know his name.
Happens that everything was packed, the whole town.
This was a Jewish little town.
That was the town where the first president, Chaim Weizmann was born.
Motala.
M-O-T-A-L-A, not far from Pinsk.
And they packed everything, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some horses and what they call it?
Carts, yes.
And we were back, and that was one of-- that-- that wasn't not far from the station.
That was a special one, wagon-- wagon, yes?
It was prepared for, them and everything was packed in.
And so we were with the-- on the-- the train, we went to Vilna.
And the next day the annexations happened, that Lithuania took over Vilna, and the whole part of [NON-ENGLISH], they called us.
When I came to Vilna, I remember [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my father mentioned to me once a name of Mr.
Lichtenstein.
And he was practicing under him, that he is occupying a very high post in a tannery.
Salman's tannery.
S-A-L-M-A-N.
So I remember that he told me this, because when I finished gymnasium, my father and I was trying to get to start an engineering something to do.
So my father told me, listen, it would be a good thing if you will go, because practically I can see you are very gifted in tanning, and so on.
It would be of interest that you would do-- you will become a master tanner.
And he mentioned that name Lichtenstein.
And I remember when I came to Vilna, I remember that I missed that he told me about this, that he's a famous tanner now.
And so I remembered this and I find out in the phone book Mr.
Lichtenstein.
I ring him, I ring him in the factory.
So he was under the impression that he's talking not to me, but to my father.
I told him, no, that is the son.
And five minutes, don't move, I am-- I'm coming.
Don't move.
I give him the address where I was there, and he came, picked me up.
Went-- didn't went back to the factory, but we went to his home.
I told him again, it's a lot, I can see that the war will take a long time and I want to do something in my profession.
So he ring to one factory, Omeiga-- the owner was Klembocka.
Could you spell the name of factory and the name of the owner, please?
Omeiga, O-M-E-I-G-A, Omeiga.
That was the firm.
And the owner was Klembocka.
K-L-E-M-B-O-C-K-A, yes, Klembocka.

And he took the phone, he ring him, and told him, would you be willing to take on as a master tanner-- so he taught-- because my father was known in Poland-- he took-- he thought also that it's is my father, not it's the son.
So yes, yes, we need somebody.
We need somebody.
And we went to him, and that took maybe a half an hour, and I got the contract with them, that for my services I was getting 1,000 litas.
That was like $1,000 a month.
And he engaged me, and I was working for them till the Lithuania was annexed, as the 13th-- the old Baltic lands was annexed to Russia, as the 13th, 14th, 15th republic.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] What they done, they nationalized the old factories.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And nationalizing, they made from the old tanneries, one combined.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the Russians put me on head as a technical manager on the old tanneries, there.
No, I wouldn't.
No, no, I don't remember.
When the annexation was, and everything was nationalized, of course, and after the form had combined of the old tanneries, that was one big tannery.
I was living-- in-- what?
Before I married?
Before I married I-- [NON-ENGLISH].
The number, I would have to find.
On my own.
I was on my own, I was by a family, Arluk, Arluk.
The name of the-- Arluk.
A-R-L-U-K.
I was renting a room, yes.
It was all right.
It was all right.
I was earning a lot of money.
I helped a lot of [NON-ENGLISH].
I got, also, my correspondence through Poles, good friends, my father's friends.
I got the correspondence with my father.
I understand that the life wasn't as that should be, because that was-- the Germans had been there-- and that the Germans had bee there.
And-- and because I helped a lot of people that-- people that my father wrote the letters, amongst them was the daughter of the director of the tannery in Radom, [INAUDIBLE].
His name-- the director's name was Jak.
She was in Vilna with a friend, Dr-- I forgot the name of the doctor.
But he was a doctor in the hospital, from the [NON-ENGLISH]-- Kasimir.
And I was fine them-- find them, take them, to be able to leave.
There was another one, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Chelmecka-- Chelmecka.
She was the wife of one of a captain from the Polish army.
And she was with her daughter.
I helped them, too.
Chelmecka, C-H-E-L-M-E-C-K-A.
Financially, because I was in earning so much money.
But before, when Lithuania was free, not involved in the war, and I was working for that firm, Omeiga.
So I was able to help people.
Yes, I helped, also, I would like also to come back to other people, like from Radom, that they have been, that was one Dudek.
D-U-D-E-K, Golberg, G-O-L-B-E-R-G, Golberg.
It was another [NON-ENGLISH], because they were from Radom.
And they know me from Radom.
So I was helping him a lot.
His brother is still alive in Israel.
Dudek Golberg, was his name, Moses Goldberg-- [NON-ENGLISH] Golberg.
The brother is still alive in Tel Aviv.
Yes.
From my savings, mostly, because I earned a lot of money for my own saving at that time.
I helped them, really, and my home was open for them.
I was married in 1941, two months before the invasion of the Germans.
My wife was Alla.
Lichtenstein.
That was the daughter of the gentleman who was-- [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] who helped me when I came to Vilna.
I was living in-- in [NON-ENGLISH], in two rooms by a Polish family, and I was working at that time for the-- when the Germans took over, that combined.
I was-- before, I was working for the nationalists combined.
